Name: spectral_curvature_routing
Title: Spectral Curvature-Aware Precision Routing for Efficient Inference
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that the curvature of the loss landscape, as measured by the spectral decay rate of the Hessian matrix, can serve as a direct proxy for precision allocation in mixed-precision inference, enabling the development of a novel, training-free precision routing framework that dynamically allocates numerical precision based on real-time activation geometry and curvature diagnostics.
Related Work: Our proposal builds upon recent advances in spectral quantization routing and geometric signal separation, but differs in its focus on curvature-aware precision allocation and the use of spectral decay rates as a proxy for quantization sensitivity. Unlike existing methods, our approach explicitly quantifies the impact of curvature on inference stability and computational difficulty, providing a more nuanced understanding of the trade-offs between precision, latency, and accuracy.
Abstract: We propose a novel, training-free precision routing framework that dynamically allocates numerical precision based on real-time activation geometry and curvature diagnostics. By computing the spectral decay rate of the Hessian matrix and mapping it to a precision-switching policy, our framework enforces strict latency budgets while preserving the optimization landscape's geometry. We theoretically ground our approach in PAC-Bayes bounds, linking spectral conditioning to generalization stability under quantization noise. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across diverse benchmarks, our method achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to static and heuristic mixed-precision baselines.
Experiments: Evaluate the proposed framework on a range of benchmarks, including WILDS and ImageNet, to demonstrate its effectiveness in achieving a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier.
Compare the performance of our framework with existing mixed-precision baselines, including static and heuristic approaches, to highlight the benefits of curvature-aware precision allocation.

Investigate the impact of different curvature diagnostics, such as the spectral decay rate and condition number, on inference stability and computational difficulty, to refine our understanding of the trade-offs between precision, latency, and accuracy.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The proposed framework relies on the accuracy of curvature diagnostics, which may be sensitive to noise and outliers in the activation data.
The computational overhead of computing spectral decay rates and mapping them to a precision-switching policy may be non-negligible, potentially impacting the overall latency of the framework.
